text: Welcome to another installment of robotic hernia surgery. Today, we're going to be reviewing a case of robotic femoral hernia repair in a woman.
This was a woman in her seventies who presented with chronic groin pain and a bulge. She had had an ultrasound performed by her PCP, which demonstrated findings consistent with a femoral hernia.
In terms of case setup for inguinal hernias done robotically, I like to have my ports triangulated to the intended groin.
I like to also make sure that I'm off midline so I don't put any large trochars through a diastasis or the linea alba, reducing potential for incisional hernia.
Upon entry and docking, the robotic system reveals the anatomical findings consistent with a femoral hernia.
The surgeon creates a peritoneal flap and dissects medially towards the pubis, revealing underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection, initially outside the correct plane, and adjusts to enter the visceral compartment of the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon approaches the femoral hernia site, preparing to begin the reduction process.
Anytime I have to work on a femoral hernia, I like to at least develop some of this retropubic dissection in advance, and then some of my lateral dissection again to
almost have that volcano sign that we look for on ventral hernias.
The surgeon, having completed the medial and lateral dissection, focuses on the femoral hernia, preparing to address it within the surgical field.
The surgical field reveals preperitoneal fat and lymphatic tissue, as the surgeon prepares to reduce the hernia sac.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the hernia sac, favoring the medial side of the dissection to avoid injury to the femoral vein, while reducing the chronic incarcerated fat from the space. A pseudo stack appearance is visible.
The surgeon carefully navigates around lymphatic tissue in the femoral hernia region, avoiding excessive dissection to prevent the formation of a lymphocele.
The surgeon performs a thorough dissection inferiorly, identifying the obturator space and reducing pre-peritoneal fat in the area.
I think in these scenarios in particular, getting a good dissection to include the obturator canal is nice to assure good mesh coverage.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the myoperitoneal orifice and transsects the round ligament, visibly separating these structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon parietalizes tissue, creating a space for the mesh to lay smoothly against the lateral sidewall.
The surgeon completes the lateral dissection, identifying the iliopsoas muscle. The femoral vein and obturator nerve are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon prepares to place a contoured mesh in the surgical site, noting the absence of an indirect hernia.
The surgeon prepares a 15 by 10 centimeter mid-weight polypropylene mesh for placement, ensuring it is suitable for covering the dissected area.
The surgeon fixates the mesh at Cooper's ligament, ensuring that the fixation does not involve tight knots.
The surgeon uses a bipolar device to achieve hemostasis, preventing bleeding during the placement of sutures for mesh fixation.
The surgeon performs a standard peritoneal flap closure from lateral to medial using an absorbable V-lock suture, ensuring the flap is securely closed.

Straightforward case. These are all, of course, outpatient procedures, and this patient did very well. Again, femoral hernias in women. Gold standard now is a MIS repair. I hope that this was helpful. Thank you.
